query: effectiveness of tecovirimat for treating smallpox

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Prior to eradication, supportive care was the primary treatment available. In the post-eradication era, development of anti-orthopoxvirus medical therapies remains an active area of research. In 2018, the United States approved tecovirimat as the first antiviral therapy indicated for treatment of smallpox. [9] Although the antiviral has only been evaluated fully in animal models, it has been administered to human volunteers in a safety trial, and as an emergency investigational drug to patients who developed post-vaccination complications. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccination has been successful in the eradication of smallpox globally. There have been several smallpox vaccines developed over time, beginning with variolation, the deliberate inoculation of infectious smallpox from the pustule of an infectious person to a healthy, nonimmune contact to induce a more mild disease course. Descriptions of variolation are found from as early as 1500 BC. This practice carried significant risk including severe disease and mortality as well as the potential for community spread of infection. In 1798, Edward Jenner’s publication of his research confirming that cowpox protected against smallpox infection led to the adoption of cowpox vaccination. By 1900, cowpox was no longer the virus used for vaccination, but vaccinia virus, which is more closely related to horsepox. Vaccinia became the virus used for large-scale global vaccination programs during the 20th century, but potential risks persisted. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The oral DNA polymerase inhibitor brincidofovir, oral intracellular viral release inhibitor tecovirimat, and intravenous vaccinia immune globulin have unknown efficacy against the mpox virus. [25] Dual therapy with tecovirimat and brincidofovir can be trialed in severe cases. Tecovirimat inhibits viral envelope protein VP37, thus blocking viral maturation as well as the release of the virus from infected cells. [32] Brincidofovir is approved for the treatment of smallpox in the US. Normal saline and probenecid should be given concurrently with cidofovir. [32] Vaccinia Immune Globulin (VIG) is licensed by the FDA to treat complications of vaccinia vaccination. [33] The effectiveness of VIG against smallpox and mpox is uncertain, and VIG has not been trialed in humans for smallpox or mpox. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Smallpox is the first human infectious disease to be successfully eradicated worldwide. It remains of clinical concern because of the potential for release and weaponization. Smallpox is a member of the viral family poxvirus, genus orthopoxvirus, and species variola virus. This disease presents with a non-specific febrile prodrome of high fever, chills, vomiting, abdominal pain, headache, and backache. The skin lesions occur 1-3 days later and first begin on the forearms or face and spread to the rest of the body. This activity illustrates the evaluation and treatment of smallpox and describes the role of the interprofessional team in managing those with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Biological Weapon Toxicity -- Clinical Significance -- Smallpox
retrieved_docs.content: The overall mortality of smallpox is approximately 30% in unvaccinated individuals. Smallpox vaccination within 72 hours of exposure can prevent the disease; vaccination within 3 to 7 days will not prevent the disease but will limit symptoms. [33] Cidofovir is theorized to be a treatment for short-term prophylaxis against smallpox. Consult the CDC for treatment recommendations.

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Monkeypox
retrieved_docs.content: Tecovirimat, an antiviral therapy that is US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved for the treatment of smallpox, is recommended for pregnant people who have severe monkeypox disease. Monkeypox can result in severe disease in pregnant people and their newborns. The WHO recommends administration of the smallpox vaccine, eg, modified vaccinia Ankara–Bavarian Nordic (MVA-BN), which contains live, nonreplicating virus, to pregnant people who are at high risk of monkeypox. Smallpox is another orthopox virus, and cross-immunity to monkeypox is expected with vaccination. Further research is needed before clinical guidelines and policies for monkeypox in pregnancy are developed. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the epidemiology of small pox. Review the presentation of a patient with small pox. Summarize the treatment of small pox. Outline the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team members to be aware of the clinical presentation of smallpox and report it efficiently to prevent an outbreak of this disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Post-eradication, vaccine researchers have utilized improved technology to develop tissue-culture-based live vaccines, live attenuated virus vaccines, and viral subunit vaccine products.  These are necessary to improve vaccine safety within the global context of an eradicated infection. Current smallpox vaccine recommendations include only personnel at increased special risk of exposure such as researchers, some healthcare workers, and some United States military personnel. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Poxviruses -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Because smallpox has mortality linked to hemorrhagic and hemodynamic instability, septicemic superinfection, and cardiogenic shock, regular interval vitals, blood counts, and coagulation profiles should be taken.

retrieved_docs.title: Poxviruses -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tecovirimat is the only approved therapeutic drug to treat orthopoxvirus infection and has shown promising activity against every Orthopoxviridae (including variola) - impeding viral progression in vitro as well as in several animal models with their respective poxviruses. It has shown to be well tolerated and very specific to the orthopoxvirus clade. It is a potent inhibitor of p37, a highly conserved and unique protein required for the formation and egress of the poxvirus extracellular virion envelope, an essential component for poxvirus spread. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there are no specific clinically proven treatments for mpox infection. As with most viral illnesses, the treatment is supportive symptom management. There are, however, prevention measures that can help prevent an outbreak. The infected individual should remain in isolation, wear a surgical mask, and keep lesions covered as much as reasonably possible until all lesion crusts have naturally fallen off and a new skin layer has formed. For severe cases, investigational use can be considered for compounds with demonstrated benefit against orthopoxviruses in animal studies and severe vaccinia vaccine complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Smallpox Generalized vaccinia Disseminated zoster Chickenpox Eczema herpeticum Disseminated herpes simplex Syphilis Yaws Scabies Rickettsialpox Measles Bacterial skin infections Drug-associated eruption [9] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccine-associated adverse events from live vaccinia virus can be serious and include generalized vaccinia, eczema vaccinatum, progressive vaccinia, and postvaccinial encephalitis. Vaccine adverse events can also cause symptoms in others through contact with vaccine site and unintended inoculation, or from vaccinated mother to fetus. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Smallpox -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Remaining variola virus stock has been restricted to the WHO Collaborating Centers on Smallpox and other Poxvirus Infections at the CDC and the Russian State Research Centre of Virology and Biotechnology (SRC VB VECTOR) labs. After the events of Sept. 11, 20001 in the United States, the concern arose for the deliberate release of the virus as an act of bioterrorism, and research for medical countermeasures including vaccines and anti-viral medications was renewed. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Mpox (Monkeypox) -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Mpox (Monkeypox) is a zoonotic orthopoxvirus that incidentally causes disease in humans similar to smallpox, although with notably lower mortality. This virus is clinically relevant because it is endemic to western and central Africa, with outbreaks in the Western Hemisphere related to the exotic pet trade and international travel. Coincident immunity to the mpox virus was previously achieved with vaccinia vaccination; however, eradicating smallpox and subsequent lack of vaccination efforts paved the way for monkeypox to gain clinical relevance. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of monkeypox infections in humans and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition and minimizing disease outbreaks.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 290
retrieved_docs.content: Brincidofovir and Tecovirimat are antiviral agents that may be used to treat smallpox (الجدري). Smallpox is classified as a viral infection, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of diseases caused by viruses. Recognizing smallpox as a viral infection highlights the relevance of antiviral therapies such as brincidofovir and tecovirimat in its management, supporting their consideration in treatment strategies for this orthopoxvirus disease.

retrieved_docs.content: to DAAs, a distinct group of nucleosides and nucleotides that are not reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors comprises brincidofovir, brivudine and remdesivir. Brivudine is the brominated analogue of deoxyuridine (bromodeoxyuridine). Remdesivir is employed to treat viral infections, whereas ribavirin is used for viral prophylaxis. For orthopoxvirus disease, both brincidofovir and tecovirimat have demonstrated efficacy against smallpox (الجدري), illustrating how specific nucleoside analogues are linked structurally and therapeutically across the broader category of viral infections. Understanding these chemical relationships, class memberships, therapeutic indications and contraindications is essential for effective management of viral diseases and optimization of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 239
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir, cidofovir, brincidofovir, and brivudine are all classified as nucleosides and nucleotides that are not reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors. These agents belong to the broader category of nucleic‑acid‑synthesis inhibitors, reflecting their mechanism of action that interferes with viral nucleic acid production. Specifically, ganciclovir’s antiviral activity is mediated through inhibition of nucleic acid synthesis. Brivudine is chemically related to bromodeoxyuridine, as indicated by its mapping to that compound. In the context of viral diseases, brincidofovir has been identified as a potential therapeutic option for smallpox (الجدري), and tecovirimat is also recognized for its ability to treat smallpox. Smallpox itself is categorized as a viral infection, underscoring the relevance of nucleic‑acid‑synthesis inhibitors and antiviral agents such as brincidofovir and tecovirimat in the management of this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic group identified as “Other antivirals in ATC” comprises the agents enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, tecovirimat and tilorone, all of which are classified together as antiviral medications. Within this group, tecovirimat is specifically recognized for its activity against smallpox (الجدري), and brincidofovir is also reported to have potential efficacy in treating smallpox. Smallpox itself is categorized as a viral infection, linking these antiviral agents to the broader context of viral disease management. Consequently, the inclusion of these drugs in the “Other antivirals in ATC” class underscores their relevance for treating viral infections, particularly for therapeutic strategies aimed at smallpox.

Clinically, gemifloxacin may treat granulomatous pneumonia, a pulmonary complication that can follow influenza infection; zanamivir provides prophylaxis against influenza. Smallpox, an orthopoxvirus disease, can be treated with brincidofovir or tecovirimat. Together, these classifications, molecular targets, mechanisms of action, contraindications, and formulation details constitute an integrated framework for antiviral therapy, anticoagulation management, antifungal treatment, and peptide‑based diagnostic imaging in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors [MoA]” defines a mechanism of action that blocks viral nucleic‑acid synthesis, and several antiviral agents belong to this class. Ganciclovir is a member of the broader category “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” and its antiviral effect is mediated through nucleic‑acid synthesis inhibition. The same class also includes cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, and remdesivir, all of which act as nucleoside or nucleotide analogues that interfere with viral DNA or RNA polymerases. Brivudine is chemically mapped to bromodeoxyuridine. Remdesivir, as a member of this nucleoside/nucleotide group, may be used to treat viral infection (عدوى فيروسية). Ribavirin is noted for its ability to prevent viral infection. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat have demonstrated activity against smallpox (الجدري), a disease that is classified as a viral infection (has parent viral infection → الجدري). This interconnected network shows how agents sharing the nucleic‑acid synthesis inhibition mechanism are employed to treat or prevent a variety of viral diseases, ranging from common viral infections to specific orthopoxvirus illnesses such as smallpox, underscoring the importance of understanding their class membership, mechanisms, and therapeutic indications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: The class “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors” includes the antiviral agents cidofovir, brincidofovir, and brivudine. Brivudine is chemically mapped to bromodeoxyuridine, indicating a structural relationship. Both brincidofovir and the antiviral tecovirimat have been identified as potential treatments for smallpox (الجدري), which is categorized as a viral infection. Recognizing the membership of these nucleoside analogues in this drug class and their possible efficacy against smallpox enhances understanding of antiviral therapy and supports informed patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 229
retrieved_docs.content: strongly associated with needle sharing; a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents (NRTIs, NNRTIs, integrase inhibitors and cyclic amines) provides options for both prophylaxis and early therapeutic regimens. Remdesivir is indicated for the treatment of viral infections. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are antiviral medications used to treat smallpox. Zanamivir is employed for influenza prophylaxis. Gemifloxacin, a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is used to manage coccidioidal pneumonia—a fungal lung infection that can arise as a secondary complication of influenza. Collectively, this network of antiviral, antiretroviral, ophthalmologic and antibacterial agents illustrates a pharmacologic strategy that interferes with viral replication at multiple enzymatic steps while also addressing associated bacterial or fungal complications, thereby supporting tailored patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: Famciklovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridine, trifluridine and vidarabine are ophthalmic antivirals employed for eye‑related viral infections. Ganciclovir is additionally classified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor and belongs to the broader “nucleosides and nucleotides (excluding reverse transcriptase inhibitors)” group, which also comprises cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, remdesivir and vidarabine. Idoxuridine, a deoxyuridine derivative, is a member of this nucleoside/nucleotide class and may prevent viral infection. Vidarabine is linked to fludarabine, indicating a structural or functional relationship. Brivudine is chemically identical to bromodeoxyuridine. Remdesivir, another nucleoside analogue, may treat viral infection. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are indicated for the treatment of smallpox, a specific viral infection. This network of relationships demonstrates how ophthalmic antivirals and nucleoside‑analogue agents are grouped by mechanism of action and therapeutic indication, providing a framework for selecting appropriate antiviral therapy for general viral infections and smallpox.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals, ophthalmologic is a therapeutic class that includes the agents famciklovir and ganciklovir, as well as the drugs trifluridin and vidarabin. Ganciklovir is categorized under the mechanism‑of‑action group Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors and is also a member of the class Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors, a group that likewise contains cidofovir, brincidofovir, and brivudine. Vidarabin belongs to both the ophthalmologic antiviral class and the nucleosides‑and‑nucleotides class, and the antineoplastic agent fludarabine is chemically mapped to vidarabin. Brivudine is synonymous with bromodeoxyuridine. Remdesivir is a member of the nucleosides and nucleotides class and has demonstrated potential to treat viral infections. Ribavirin may be used to prevent viral infections. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are indicated for the treatment of smallpox (الجدري), which itself is a specific type of viral infection. This network of classifications, mechanisms, and therapeutic indications illustrates the interconnected nature of antiviral agents and their roles in managing viral diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 232
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals, ophthalmologic comprise a therapeutic group that includes famciklovir and ganciklovir as well as trifluridin and vidarabin, reflecting their use in eye infections. Ganciklovir functions as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, indicating that its antiviral activity is mediated by disruption of viral DNA replication, and it is classified within the broader category of nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors. Vidarabin (vidarabin) also belongs to this nucleoside/nucleotide class, and fludarabine is chemically mapped to vidarabin, underscoring a structural relationship between these agents. Additional members of the nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors class are cidofovir, brincidofovir, and brivudine; brivudine is specifically mapped to bromodeoxyuridin, its brominated nucleoside analogue. Both brincidofovir and tecovirimat have been identified as potential treatments for الجدري (smallpox), a disease that is categorized as a viral infection. This network of relationships highlights how ophthalmic antivirals, nucleoside analogues, and specific antiviral agents intersect through shared classifications, mechanisms of action, chemical mappings, and therapeutic indications for viral diseases such as smallpox.

Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are antiviral agents indicated for smallpox, and zanamivir is used prophylactically against influenza, a viral respiratory illness that can progress to pneumonia with nodular infiltrates. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured taxonomy of antiviral, antibacterial, antifungal, anticoagulant, radiopharmaceutical and immunomodulatory agents organized by chemical class, mechanism of action, and clinical indication, thereby supporting precise prescribing, safety monitoring, and diagnostic use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, as are cyclic amines with antiviral activity and nucleosides and nucleotides that do not act as reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors. Within these classes, ribavirin is a member of the HCV‑direct‑acting antiviral group, while cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, and remdesivir belong to the nucleoside/nucleotide subclass that excludes reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors. Brivudine is chemically related to bromodeoxyuridine. Rimantadine, which is a cyclic‑amine direct‑acting antiviral, exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Therapeutically, remdesivir may be used to treat viral infections, and ribavirin may help prevent viral infections. Both brincidofovir and tecovirimat have activity against smallpox, a disease that is itself a type of viral infection. This network of relationships highlights how various direct‑acting antiviral agents are grouped by chemical structure and mechanism, and how specific agents are linked to particular viral diseases, informing both drug classification and clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 119
retrieved_docs.content: Ophthalmic antivirals comprise several nucleoside analogues that are used to treat viral eye infections. The group “Antivirals, ophthalmologic” includes famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridine and trifluridine. Ganciclovir functions as a nucleic‑acid‑synthesis inhibitor and is classified among “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” a broader category that also contains cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine and remdesivir. Idoxuridine is derived from deoxyuridine (deoxyuridin has child idoxuridin) and is reported to may prevent عدوى فيروسية (viral infection). Brivudine is chemically related to bromodeoxyuridine, as indicated by its mapping to that structure. Within the same nucleoside‑nucleotide class, remdesivir is recognized for its potential to treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection). Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are both noted for their ability to treat الجدري (smallpox), and smallpox itself is a specific type of viral infection (has parent viral infection). This network of relationships highlights how several ophthalmic antiviral agents share mechanistic and structural ties with broader antiviral nucleoside/nucleotide drugs, and how some of these agents extend their therapeutic use beyond ocular disease to systemic viral infections such as COVID‑19 (remdesivir) and smallpox (brincidofovir, tecovirimat). Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antiviral therapies based on drug class, mechanism of action, and the specific viral pathogen involved.
